title: Cross-Functional Friction

content.question: Where do tasks stall inside your business? Between which two people or functions does work die the longest?
sop: Map your core workflow as a chain of handoffs (e.g., sales to fulfillment, design to production).
For your last 3 projects, note where each one stalled longest and for how long.
Rate Handoff Delay, Rework, and Misalignment 1-10 for your weakest handoff, then sum them (see calculation, page 5)
Assign one clear owner on each side of that handoff and define what done-and-ready-to-pass actually means.
Re-check the same handoff in 30 days and see if the delay shrank.
content.explainer: Teams don't fail at their own work, they fail at the seams between their work and someone else's.
questions: Where do tasks stall inside your business? Between which two people or functions does work die the longest?
Which handoff in your workflow requires the most back-and-forth before anyone's satisfied with the result?
If you mapped your last big project's timeline, which gap between two people ate the most days?
content.equation_example: Rate Handoff Delay, Rework, and Misalignment 1-10 each for your weakest handoff, then add them. Handoff Delay 7, Rework 6, Misalignment 5 → CFF = 7+6+5 = 18 out of a possible 30. Higher CFF means more value is leaking at the seams between teams; lower means handoffs are clean and fast.
content.closing_statement: The seams cost more than the work itself.

caption:
Principle Nº 18: Cross-Functional Friction

Most problems are between teams. Teams don't fail at their own work, they fail at the seams between their work and someone else's.

Ask yourself: Where do tasks stall inside your business? Between which two people or functions does work die the longest?
